request.question: Imiliya is a town located in what pilgrimage site?

title: Kushinagar
Pushkar
Dobbaspet
Gajpanth
Kundalpur
Lumbini
Imiliya
Chakia, Bihar
Giruwari
Grishneshwar
sentences: Kushinagar, Kusinagar or Kusinara is a pilgrimage town and a Notified Area Council in the Kushinagar district of the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh located around NH-28, and is 52 km east of Gorakhpur city.
It is an important Buddhist pilgrimage site, where Buddhists believe Gautama Buddha attained Parinirvana after his death.
Outside India, it is an International Buddhist Pilgrimage Centre.
The followers of Buddhism, especially from Asian countries, wish to visit this place at least once in their lifetime.
Pushkar (Hindi: पुष्कर ) is a town in the Ajmer district in the Indian state of Rajasthan.
It is situated 14 km northwest of Ajmer at an average elevation of 510 m and is one of the five sacred dhams (pilgrimage site) for devout Hindus.

According to Hindu theology, the pond at the Katas Raj Temples near Choa Saidan Shah in Chakwal District of Pakistan has a theological association with Shiva; it was formed by the tears of Lord Shiva which he is believed to have shed after the death of his wife, Sati.

The story goes that when Sati died, Shiva cried so much and for so long, that his tears created two holy ponds – one at Pushkara in Ajmer in India and the other at Ketaksha, which literally means raining eyes, in Sanskrit.
It is from this name that the word Katas is derived.
It is often called "Tirth Raj" – the king of pilgrimage sites – and has in recent years become a popular destination for foreign tourists.
Dobbspet is a tiny town located in Nelamangala Taluk, Bangalore Rural District, Karnataka, India.

formerly called as Somapura re named as Dobbspet in memory of Major General Richard Stewart Dobbs first collector of Tumkur District, who is an eminent statesman who devoted his major part of life for the service of the People by the establishment of order and systematic administration in Tumkur district.
Dobbspet is located 50 km away from Bangalore along National Highway 4.
Nearest city is Tumkur.
Hindu pilgrimage site of Shivagange lies near the town.
Gajpanth (also spelled "Gajpantha") is a Jain pilgrimage site ("Sidh-ak-shetra") located near the Jain temple at Nashik.
It is located in Masrul village, in the Nashik district of the Indian state of Maharashtra.
It is 16 km from the Nashik Road Railway station and 5 km from Nashik City, and is situated on the steep slopes of a 400 ft hill.
The pilgrimage site is accessible via a staircase built in black stone, which leads directly to the temple.
The hill has 450 steps, three caves (known as "' chamar leni' "), and several temples belonging to the Digambara sect of the Jains.
There is also a sculpture depicting samavasarana (divine preaching hall of tirthankara) on the hills of Gajpanth.
Kundalpur is a town located in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh, 35 km from the city of Damoh.
It is a historical pilgrimage site for Jains in India.
Lumbinī (Nepali and Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी    , "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi District of Nepal.
It is the place where, according to Buddhist tradition, Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.
Gautama, who achieved Enlightenment some time around 528 BCE, became the Buddha and founded Buddhism.
Lumbini is one of many magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha.
Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal.
Chakia is one of the important and commercial towns and a Nagar Panchayat in Purvi Champaran district in the state of Bihar, India.
Chakia is sub divisional headquarter town and also block headquarter.
During the British rule, Champaran Sugar Mill was founded in 1905.
sugar cane mill is now defunct since 1995.
Chakia is one of the many gateways to nearby villages.
Cloths and food grain traded between near by villages and Chakia.
Chakia now is a rapidly growing town.

Chakia has many old and famous places of worship like Shree Rani Sati Mandir, Baudhi Devi Mandir, Balkhana Devi Mandir, Durga Mandir, Maruti Mandir (Bife Bazaar), Hanumaan Mandir (Bara Bazaar), Noori Masjid (Near Railway Station), Jama Masjid and many more.
The town down area of chakia are Keshria Road, Sahebganj Road, Station Road, Motihari Road, Hospital Road and Madhuban Road.
There areas are densely populated with small shops as well as branded or non-branded shop selling various types of goods and services.
The associate Post Office is called Bara Chakia.
This town is served by a broad gauge train and connected by NH-28.
The railway station is called Chakia.
It is also the closest railway station to Kesaria Stupa, a Buddhist pilgrimage site.
Giruwari sometimes known as Girwari Jhyalbas ( गिरुवारी ) is one of the village located in north part of Nawalparasi district of Lumnini Zone in the western development region of Nepal.
Lumbinī (Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी, "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi district of Nepal.
[1] It is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama, who is the Buddha.
Gautama founded the Buddhist tradition.
Buddha lived between roughly 563 and 483 BC.
Lumbini is one of four magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha, the others being at Kushinagar, Bodh Gaya and Sarnath.
Grishneshwar temple, sometimes referred to as the Ghrneshwar Jyotirlinga or Dhushmeshwar temple, is one of the 12 Jyotirlinga shrines mentioned in the Shiva Purana.
The word "Ghrneshwar" means "lord of compassion".
The temple is an important pilgrimage site in Shaivism tradition of Hinduism, which considers it as the last or twelfth Jyotirlinga (linga of light).
This pilgrimage site is located in Ellora (also called Verul), less than a kilometer from Ellora Caves – a UNESCO World Heritage site.
It is about 30 km north-west of the city of Aurangabad, and about 300 km east-northeast from Mumbai.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Imiliya is a town located in what pilgrimage site?
hop1_docs: Imiliya | Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal.
Madhu, Sri Lanka | Madhu is town in Mannar District, Sri Lanka.  The Shrine of Our Lady of Madhu, a major site of Roman Catholic pilgrimage, is located in this town.

Jison-in | Jison-in (慈尊院 ) is a Buddhist temple in the town of Kudoyama that marks the entrance to the pilgrimage route of Koyasan.  It is part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Kushinagar | Kushinagar, Kusinagar or Kusinara is a pilgrimage town and a Notified Area Council in the Kushinagar district of the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh located around NH-28, and is 52 km east of Gorakhpur city.  It is an important Buddhist pilgrimage site, where Buddhists believe Gautama Buddha attained Parinirvana after his death.  Outside India, it is an International Buddhist Pilgrimage Centre.  The followers of Buddhism, especially from Asian countries, wish to visit this place at least once in their lifetime.
Kundalpur | Kundalpur is a town located in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh, 35 km from the city of Damoh.  It is a historical pilgrimage site for Jains in India.

Yoshino Mountain | Yoshino Mountain (吉野山 , Yoshino-yama ) is a mountain located in the town of Yoshino in Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture, Japan.  In 2004, It was designated as part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site under the name "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range".

Reward, Saskatchewan | 12 km south of Reward on Grid 675 is the site of the Shrine of the Holy Rosary. .  The site has been the location of an annual pilgrimage since 1932. About 3,000 people participated in the first pilgrimage.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Imiliya — [1]: "Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town"
- Kapilvastu District — [1]: "Kapilvastu District"
- Lumbini — [1]: "Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal"

Ambiguities:
- None found for "Imiliya" or "Lumbini" in the provided passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing relation: passage [1] explicitly states Imiliya's location as in "Lumbini". All required facts to answer the question are present and unambiguous.

Retrieval direction:
- "Lumbini" (page)
- "Imiliya Kapilvastu District Lumbini Nepal" (search query)
Final concise conclusion: Imiliya is located in Lumbini.
hop2_docs: Imiliya | Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal.

Lumbini | Lumbinī (Nepali and Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी    , "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi District of Nepal.  It is the place where, according to Buddhist tradition, Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.  Gautama, who achieved Enlightenment some time around 528 BCE, became the Buddha and founded Buddhism.  Lumbini is one of many magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha.
Buddhist pilgrimage sites in Nepal | Lumbini is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.  There are number of historical Buddhist pilgrimage sites in Nepal.

Rishikesh Complex of Ruru Kshetra | The Rishikesh Complex of Ruru Kshetra is located in southwestern Nepal in the Palpa District, Lumbini Zone.  It is an important Hindu pilgrimage as well as cremation site referred to in the Hindu epics.

Giruwari | Giruwari sometimes known as Girwari Jhyalbas ( गिरुवारी ) is one of the village located in north part of Nawalparasi district of Lumnini Zone in the western development region of Nepal.  Lumbinī (Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी, "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi district of Nepal. [1] It is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama, who is the Buddha.  Gautama founded the Buddhist tradition.  Buddha lived between roughly 563 and 483 BC.  Lumbini is one of four magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha, the others being at Kushinagar, Bodh Gaya and Sarnath.

Aniruddha Mahathera | Aniruddha Mahathera (Devanagari: अनिरुद्ध महाथेरा) (born Gaja Ratna Tuladhar) (15 December 1915 – 17 February 2003) was a Nepalese Buddhist monk and the Sangha Nayak (Patriarch) of Nepal from 1998 until his death in 2003.  He was one of the most important figures in the revival of Theravada Buddhism in Nepal and the development of Lumbini, the Buddha's birthplace in southern Nepal, into a center of international pilgrimage.
Lumbini Sanskritik | Lumbini Sanskritik is a municipality situated in Rupandehi District of Lumbini Zone in Nepal.
response.summary_2: Imiliya is located in Lumbini, the Buddhist pilgrimage site.
